title: New Words for a New Industry | The Aboard Podcast 2026-03-31

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hi, I'm Paul Ford.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm Rich Ziade.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is the Aboard Podcast, the podcast about how AI is changing the world of software. Rich, how are you doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I ay-ay-ay-ay-ay-ay. I'm so sorry that just happened. Let's play the theme song and I want to make some dumb jokes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I love jokes!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The other day I was asked to come talk to a prominent local media organization, to their designers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Newsday?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes, Newsday. For those who don't know New York City, Newsday is a, was a really special publication actually, because it was like a mix between, it had a little tabloid to it, but it was very Long Island focused.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It was Queens, Long Island.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is why people are tuned in to this episode. They want to hear us talk about Newsday.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Don't talk about Newsday.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, Newsday was a good paper.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: A prominent New York City-based but really truly global organization.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes, yes exactly. I gave a talk. Anyways, I gave a talk and it was to designers and I have to say, you know when you are asked to give a talk, there's a little bit of an assumption that you might have some answers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You better have some answers.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I had to go up there and I had to say, it's all changing really fast and I just want to tell you, as someone who's operating a firm around AI, that I really don't know exactly what the future looks like. I don't know what the present looks like.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I just want to talk about some of the ways that categories and boundaries are blurring and collapsing. And the way I did that was by making up the dumbest words I could.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Fun.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'll give you an example. Yeah. When you're a company, you have two kinds of potential people who might buy your product or services.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, we used to run an agency. What, what are the buyers of an agency?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Clients.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right. Sure. And then if you sell a product, what's that?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Customers.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Put them together and you get clustomers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh my.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I know. But that apparently...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You ran with this?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I did. I ran with this. And then the, and you know, people laughed.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, they had to. I, I told them I got into a little bike accident on the way up. So if they didn't find me funny...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Out of sympathy.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you need to just laugh. Come on! It was bad too, like I just slammed into a car. But it wasn't, I didn't really hurt myself, it just vumpfed. Oof. It's midtown. I shouldn't be riding a bike in midtown in the middle of the day.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Certain parts. Anyway, regardless.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Chaos. That didn't work because apparently MailChimp uses clustomers as a term.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Really?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, in their, that's what people have told me.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Ah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because I workshop all this at the, at the Gotham Comedy Club. I go at 2 and I give...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Dangerfields?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I give, I give keynote decks at the Giggle Hut. Alright, so

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let's go different direction.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Customer plus client.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Client-mer?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, custoliant.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah! But the point is...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, what's your point here, Ford?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't have a point. No, I think that what's happening and I just want to put a name to it, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So most companies think of having customers for their products or having clients for their services.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But when you can deliver a product as a service along the way...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...which you can do when you can build an app in two hours.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, what is that? And what we're finding as a firm and as I've been going out and talking to more and more people, this is very common in the industry right now...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...which is, wait a minute, everything we understand—we still are providing value, we're still shipping stuff, we're still making stuff...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Uh-huh.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...but the ways and the boundaries around what we thought we were doing, like a designer designs and an engineer writes code and a product manager...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...makes them talk to each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Everybody's finding that it's all blurring, and that producing software is—it's not cheap exactly because product and thinking is still hard. Thinking's still hard, but the coding and the building is easy. And we've gone through this a bunch of times in the podcast, I don't think we need to belabor this part, but what it means is that as the news gets out, when the news first showed up it was a lot of engineers going like 'Okay, wow, I can code really fast.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I'm getting emailed to me relatively complicated code artifacts from people who write articles.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Like they're smart, they're technical.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're building software...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: ...and they're not engineers.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's functional deployed software. They want to show it to me. And it really—it's okay, like it's okay. I think one of the things I'm starting to realize is everything in this space, we're all looking for what I call the asterisk, and the asterisk is the reason where you go and you go 'well, it can never really do this one thing.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But that's not actually necessarily the right way to think about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because what happens is you basically set yourself up because then someone will be like 'Ah, you just write a certain kind of loop and now it does that thing.' It's more about where can I provide value to people who are talking to me right now? How can I move stuff along? So designer...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...you add engineer, you add product manager.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You're putting all three together.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because that's I think what you got. What do you call that? What do you call that? You call that a design-gineer manager?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, you can actually take the DE from designer, the ER from engineer, and the duct from product manager, and then that new job is called a deer duct.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Have you lost your mind?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: A little bit. What would you call the job?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I want to say something interesting instead. I want to talk about for a moment labor.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I came in with cheerful wordplay to approach a complicated subject and you say 'No, go, Robert Reich, please, right, please tell me'.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We sell two things. Two things.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Goods?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And services.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Goods is like I have chewing gum that tastes like mint.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That- that sounds delicious.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I will take care of packaging it, I'll make sure it's not poison, and I will sell it to you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Can you spend hundreds of millions of dollars on a campaign about twins?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure. Okay. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Services breaks down like this: I am an expert in, let me pick an industry, the law. You are not. You will pay me for my expertise.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: As a lawyer you like to bring this up.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I am an expert, I make custom drapery. You're a radiologist.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm a radiologist.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You are not.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, I'm not.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I will sign off on my results and my interpretation of those results. You will pay me a lot of money. And the less of me there are, the more you will pay me.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know what I hate? I hate the heavy smock.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hmm?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know when you go to the radiologist, you gotta lie down and they put that heavy smock on you?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh so the- like- radiation doesn't make it through to you?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Doesn't mess up your- your giblets. Yeah, yeah, yeah. As I was saying radiologist, I mean it's like, uh, heavy smock.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So here's what's happening. What's happening is this: the buyer of my expertise who needs a contract or a x-ray read, right? Is late at night, after dinner, not even late at night, it's eight o'clock, taking the problem that they would have taken to the expert and giving it a go.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And they're like 'Check it out, one of the dumbest things you can do is go back to your lawyer or your radiologist and say, Hey, what do you think? It's pretty good, huh?' Right? Why? Because a huge percentage of the economy is driven by the delta between my expertise and your non-expertise.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This is real.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what you've- what you're highlighting, and actually let's point out, that is the definition of white collar of high margin, right? If you run a Wendy's, the amount of money you make per hamburger is really low, it's like probably pennies or nickels, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's low margin.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And one of the reasons I think people are so anxious about AI is that that gap, that labor gap right there between where I really get to charge you a lot of money because I will deliver that much value to you from what's in my brain.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. Okay. Yes. Now, what you're finding people say, and there's skepticism when they say it is, 'That's cute'. They minimize, like engineers when they first saw like cool-looking demos coming out, they'd be like 'It's not real. It's not real. You can't put that in production. You can't- uh- rely on that. You can't run your business on that.' And that's a natural reaction. A lawyer will say 'Please do not attach that to an email. That needs to be reviewed by like a proper, experienced attorney, etc.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Is it defensive? It's actually not because this stuff has landed so fast that it's a normal reaction for either a doctor or a lawyer to say slow down. This is dangerous stuff that can cause harm and we should review it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, you know, one place to learn here is from the doctors who are like, 'Wow, this cuts down on bureaucracy.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm going to use it that way, but obviously it will not be able to diagnose and if any of my, you know, any of my students try to diagnose with it, I will hit them with a stick.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because I have this set of 100-plus years of protocols and systems. I stayed awake 72 hours in a row in order to internalize, so I'm pretty confident that I know how this needs to behave.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I think what's really tricky is in a lot of other spaces, we don't have the confidence that the doctor has.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Lawyers know how a lawyer's supposed to behave. So the minute the LLM doesn't behave like a lawyer, they go, 'Uh-uh.' But most people don't really know how a product manager is supposed to behave, including most product managers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I mean, product managers, designers, engineers, that's sort of the three-legged stool there. There has always been interdependence between their core skills and there's always been kind of a bizarre tension between all three of them, right? The product manager has effectively felt somewhat powerless, but depending on the setting, could have real authority. I mean, everybody's exhausting because they have skills.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: If you have skills and expertise, you're by default a little exhausting because you're getting lectured by people who don't have your skills.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And now the people who don't have your skills are giving it a whirl at home.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, they have skill simulators.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: If the outputs would have just plateaued, I would have been like, 'Ah, you still need the expert,' but boy they keep getting better.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know, there's an analog here in flight simulator.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Early versions of flight simulator I wouldn't necessarily put you behind the cockpit.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But current versions are pretty good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think they use them for training.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And they do, right? And actually like the big 737s and so on, they have big simulation environments.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The boundaries between being really good at flying a plane and simulating flying a plane, if you have some of the right equipment, you get the tactile feeling and you really study it, those start to blur. We've been down paths a little bit like this, it's just they're so hyperspecific.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That said, you cannot certify as a pilot in flight simulator.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We make you go up with another pilot into the sky.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I think your exercise...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: ...here of mushing words together is because those protocols between expertise have been threatened.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They are threatened.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They just are. Product managers feel, everybody's feeling empowered for the thing they've been told to stay away from because they can mess around with it. Engineers are designing, product managers are doing everything, designers are coding, all the normal sort of niceties and boundaries that existed have been kind of threatened.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So let me be clear here, I hear from a lot...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: the people about their reaction to this technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sometimes like a dozen a day. And I get emails and I get signals.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There's no one consistent response. But what is very clear to me and the reason why we've been pushing a certain direction is that the days of saying that that boundary collapse won't happen, or it will happen under certain sets of rules, and that buyers and customers will want things to be the way they were, that is fading and we can't change that, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I agree.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So it's very hard because I really, you know, every fiber of my being wants to say don't you worry. People are what matter. But I actually think it's more complicated than that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's more complicated than that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So anyway, I think like we're getting kind of into the weeds, right? But we're reacting to this just like anyone else. What we assumed is that at some point, like a really, like two years ago, we're like, we're going to make a thing, we'll put it on the web, people use it, they'll tell us if they like it or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So look, I mean, here's where we are. And I'm like, I'm actually making it, I'm just a little tuckered because I've been on a lot of podcasts recently.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, here we go. The name-dropping.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no, no, this is my job.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But you know, one of the reasons I'm smooshing together the words is just trying to make it kind of like something we can talk about. Because these words have so much sort of weight associated with them. Like I, we used to run an agency together and people would be like, oh, you're an agency. What kind of agency? What, like in, if people are in an agency,

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: People need to frame you to understand you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They really, really do. And no one can frame what's happening right now because it's changing so fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. I think what's crazy is it's not a new tech. It's not a VR headset, which is additive. It's something you introduce into culture and into social dynamics. I don't think I've ever seen anything that that has actually threatened the status quo quite like this.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Meaning it's not a new technology that you bolt on. It's something that is empowering everyone to question the way things are.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, I'll give you one example of thing that, a thing that was this threatening, which was Linux servers running web browsers in the closet. Right? We have a, we are an all Microsoft shop. The web is not to be trifled with.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We are very secure. 25,000 people hacked into our system on, you know, Windows for Workgroups in the last two hours, but doesn't matter. We're not publishing no website and the last thing we're doing is letting some Unix machine, open source, come into our system.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And actually what happened is nerds kept setting them up. They kept setting up these little servers with free software and then it was sort of the web bloomed on those.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what happens now is you can run Linux inside of Windows. Like eventually it kind of all reconverges. But at the moment, they saw that as existential threat because it was going to take their market share. Because it was unimaginable that things would get too much bigger than they already were.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: If I've got a pretty good spot in the status quo and a threat shows up...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...which was Microsoft in the 90s. That was the status quo.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And then again later, Microsoft was threatened multiple times. Yeah. But let's go back to the individual or a particular profession is being threatened and whatnot. There are certain tactics you do to sort of preserve your place. You have to try to preserve your place because there's—you gotta pay for college tuition. Yes. Like it's just normal stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So here's humans and I—the term I keep coming up with is...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I love it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: ...it's like we have a product and we are an agency and you put that together and you get a pragency.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Pragency.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you put a Y in there. P-R-A-Y. Put a Y in there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, because that's where we're at. I mean, let's pause on that for a second. I—I think over a year ago, I forget who, I think it might have been Noah Brier who said SaaS is software as a service and I think he flipped it or maybe it was you who said service as a software.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't know who said anything anymore. Could have been Noah. Could have been me.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think the rigidity of products that you buy and or lease or license is being called into question because you assume that those require massive investment and they're requiring a lot less of it today. So much of the assumptions we made are being turned upside down. That's just reality. You got another one for me?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, this is the worst because what they really want for the company—the services-oriented platform company to become—is just an agency but you put a T in there. It's the agent-cy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you know what that's called?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Agentrification.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh boy. Yeah. So I've been working hard.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This is tough ball.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I know. Sorry. We're going to stop that because I really thought that would be fun and light. All right, I agree with you. I like what you're doing here because you're clever, you're a clever guy.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, thanks.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You essentially are highlighting that the walls and the boundaries—which by the way, you're making a joke of it, but boundaries are oftentimes and enforcement of boundaries is how people make a living, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know why I'm making—that's why I'm making a joke about it because it's so real and I'm seeing it happen and people are absolutely pissed. They're so pissed.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Are they? Oh God, yeah. Like explain.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Software could never do that. Right? It's very threatening. This is a very threatening moment to people who are—and and you don't even get direct—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Everybody?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, sure. The vibes are bad, man. Like it's a lot of stuff. Yeah, it's a lot and like the country's at war. Like it's a mess right now, alright? And so and this just is one more freaking thing that keeps coming. Yeah. I like that I keep an active LinkedIn feed of articles that are related to this subject that are interesting, right? And man, there's a lot of engagement these days. Like people and it's not my words, right? I'm just sort of like, this is interesting signal, not endorsing it or not. But it's a lot of engineers trying to—a lot of software people just trying to internalize what's going on. And there's a lot of hot—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Responses to just sort of... wow, you know, this is all a bunch of crap and just sort of like, this is LinkedIn. It's not BlueSky. It's like...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Are you surprised?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, I'm not surprised at all. That's why I'm making little jokes because it's a way to just kind of get the conversation going a little bit. You know, I would say I wouldn't give my delivery the highest marks today. You know, I think, but but you know, give me a couple more rounds at the Chuckle Hut and I'll be able to kind of land these ideas.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, I think you're hilarious.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But we got to make it possible to talk about the fact, look, one of our designers asked for a good task with Claude Code because they're learning, right? And I said make a component system and then build the full React Storybook library so that engineers could cut and paste those elements, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: React Storybook is UI components neatly organized in a catalog.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so for those who don't know what this is, this would be like designers work a whole lot on the component library and then the engineers set it up inside of React Storybook and there's a high cost of change in this world.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, the designers wouldn't work on React Storybook. They would never touch it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what happened?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Popped right up.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What popped right up?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Like two hours later he's like 'check it out'.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: On his own?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, right? So you got to like, that's an artifact that's real.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And it looked good. It looked good. I showed it to you, it looked good. Right? That's an artifact that's real that exists. Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let me close this with a question. Pretty hard one. I don't think you have the answer to it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I hope not.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. When I think about people and their expertise and how they transact with one another, it's like a currency exchange.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Often it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is a currency exchange, right? My currency as, let me be a lawyer for a second, is costs this much. Here's a running joke, like it's a known thing. Tax attorneys are the most expensive attorneys. Like real expertise around tax law is very expensive.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because they constantly kill themselves so there's only like three left.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There's not many. There's not many.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They probably have amazing lifespans.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They might have amazing lifespans.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, like that's not a job... first of all that doesn't take you... you don't do a lot of heli-skiing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let me stereotype for a second, they're rarely the rainmakers. They're kind of weird. They like the IRS code. They like to get in there. But they're very, very expensive. And the reason they're expensive is there are very few of them and there are very few people who have an appetite for tax law. Like when the new code lands, they're all over it. It's like an album dropping for them, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This is kind of the worst thing I've ever heard in my life.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Right. So now, fast forward to today. And I've done this, by the way. I've asked ChatGPT to give me some tax advice on a business that is located in a certain city, in a certain state, in a certain country.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, you're a lawyer and you're pretty good at receiving tax advice. You're an entrepreneur and a lawyer.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It wrote it in plain English. It didn't think I was a lawyer, it didn't assume I was a lawyer. And I have a call the next day with my tax lawyer.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: My tax lawyer charges $2,000 an hour. Not unusual in tax law, like higher-end corporate tax law. My perception of that person's value after my session with ChatGPT on this stuff has changed.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: How do you price? How do you adjust currency exchange in amidst all of this? How do you price? How do you price your value?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're asking me, Paul?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Not the tax lawyer, but anyone. We are—full transparency—as a shop that is as about as good as it gets in shipping software rapidly, are trying to figure this out.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. How do you price?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's the wrong question.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Ooh, you went there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's the wrong question. Nobody knows. And everybody who tells—everybody who says that they know is absolutely lying. And I'll tell you why, because at some level, even during pitches we're building. Okay, so it changed. The deal is different than it used to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The deal is different.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So how do you price refers to the old world, right? Refers to like, how do I keep a tax attorney worth $2,000? Okay, that is absolutely not the question that capitalism has ever allowed to be asked.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm okay with him getting cheaper. Fine. But what's the new—what's—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So here's the question I want you to ask every day when you wake up.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: How can I use the change in the world to drive value to the people who can most take advantage of it? To me, that often comes down to nonprofits, local press organizations, things like that. But it also is medium-sized business and it can be parts of large businesses.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The tax attorney at some level is going to have to stand up for themselves and figure out where they live, and they're going to have to identify the value they provide and this seems unfair—it seems, and it is—but they have to identify the value they provide and they have to self-commoditize. And what's happened is we have this world where everyone has been told what their ticket is and what they're going to cost, and they line up. And that's what I did. I was a consultant for many years. I was $150 an hour when things were going okay, and I got more expensive as time went on. When I was a freelance writer in my 20s, I was 50 an hour. I know my—I know my value. It's gone up. And, you know, people will do the math on that, but then you're paying your own healthcare and you're really only usually work—able to get work for about three weeks out of the month. So it sort of balances out, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Explain self-commoditize. I think that's a very exciting way to end this podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. I mean, look, you're in a market and you go and you learn how the market works. And if you've never freelanced, here—here was what was always tricky about running an agency is that people didn't know what business they were in because they were aligned with their discipline.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: When you say people, you mean people in the agency.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'll give—yes, of course, like designers, right? And this is not to pick on designers, product managers too, and engineers. So our job was to find and identify leads, bring them in, ask them for money...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: as much money as they would give us for and then turn that into a project that people would then bring their labor to it, and then if we we could if they paid us a little more than the labor cost, we could have some money.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We made we were profitable.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, after rent, you know, CapEx and OpEx, etcetera, etcetera. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So, what happens is that and I I would always advocate for this and it's really not something that people find interesting. I think this is where so much pain is coming from. Most people don't know what business they're in. They know what discipline they're in. They know that they work at an agency and they get projects from their boss.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I think when you are in your 20s, that's really normal. I'll be frank, I didn't know what business I was in half the time when I was in my 20s.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You're just doing good work.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're outputting.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I got some freelance work. But when I went freelance in as I got into my mid-20s

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, now you're now you're closer to the sun.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And there used to be a culture around freelancing where you would there was a book of rates, right? Like it was just there was an understanding in the industry. And and you'd get hired by large orgs and small orgs and they paid differently. Uh-huh. And so you developed an internalized sense of what you cost, what you could attract.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, your price is wholly dependent on what people are willing to pay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So now, so now fast-forward to this madness today.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The tax attorney, if anyone should be able to figure out what they should cost in a new world, it is a tax attorney. Mm. Right? Like what value are they going to bring? First of all,

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: if I was a tax attorney, I would double down on let me validate any of your LLM chats. Mm. As a free service to you. Let me stay in your world.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So you're telling him or her to embrace the tools.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I know I don't I'm not telling anybody to do anything. I'm just telling you that it's coming. if Rich is here, a a regular purchaser of tax strategy is sitting here telling me and the 2 million people who listen to this podcast Mm. that it's okay to get on ChatGPT.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you can be there as the tax attorney. You can be like, God, no, it's the worst ChatGPT gives the worst advice. Don't do it. Don't do it. Don't do it. But you're yelling at this podcast and it can't hear you. And everybody else is like, well, maybe I will ask it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And that's happening. It's just happening and happening and happening. I can't fix it. Nobody can fix it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And not only that, even if you had a perfect strategy for explaining why this is a really bad idea and the the, you know, you're losing the spiritual aspect of taxing. Oh. You've got multi-trillion dollar aircraft carriers coming into port.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So I it's some level, can everyone just admit, like, there may not be a perfect victory on the other side of this?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I mean, we're agency people. There are never perfect victories.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, agency is people don't understand and agency job usually cycle through in about two years. And it's pretty punishing. And the people who are good at it are freaking weird. And that's just real. Like you and I are

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: we are

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: A kind of special and painful form of business person.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I agree with your non-answer.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I do—I think a lot of your mushing words together—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's not an answer. It's figure out if okay, if the boundaries are blurring, how can you preserve what's good and drive the value? Because I swear to God, if you're really valuable and you drive the value and you don't try to hold it and you don't be the person who uses GPT and keeps telling them, like, don't ever, you know, blah—if you—if you do that, they will reward you.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I agree.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But it won't look like the billing sheet from back in the days. That's real confusing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let's pitch people. We always end this with a little bumper ad on Aboard. We've got a couple of offerings now. Do you want to tell them about them?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, this is a funny—this is the irony of the firm, right? Which is, while I am telling you I do not have answers about what's happening in culture—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but if you're going to give us money, we can give you answers.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Not about culture, not gonna pretend.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, no, no. We're not therapists here.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I can help you really quickly ship a platform on a variety of different technologies.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. I mean, this is real, right? So, like, while all this is going on, we keep honing and honing and honing our skills. And so things that I'm thinking about a project, it was related to a really, sort of, really sticky billing issue that we just unlocked, right? How long would that have taken at the agency?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Four months.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what would we charge? We can just be upfront about that price.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Half a million bucks. 600 grand.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right. And it would have been a team of five, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So how long did it take?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: A month.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: How much did it cost?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Much less.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: A fraction. A fraction, right? In fact, we finished a lot of it before we finished the contract.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. That's real.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Right. And so the—we're very good at these tools now. We have our own custom technology, we have a sort of very structured way of building and understanding data models inside of AI in order to get, like, very predictable results. And then we sort of put the—the foot on the gas at that point, and then we go to our good friend Claude Code and we say, take the—the smarts here and let's get an app.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We're experienced enterprise software people that are—that know how to use these tools to ship to production.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And we are not trying to hide the value. We are sharing the value with our customers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You can hire us and give us a goal line, and we ship software. 30, 60, 90 days. We call it Aboard Launch. And that's a very goal-driven, let's get you to production as fast as possible. Except it's—it's reliable, it's secure, we test it and everything, but it's five times faster than it used to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: In the future we'll—we'll actually talk through this process because I think our listener—our listeners will care to hear how you get a big platform in 90 days that's very reliable.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Another way you can work with us is a product offering we call Aboard Transform, which is us frankly putting our talented teams into your org helping...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: helping you sort of deal with all this change.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: and just accelerating across your org, shipping much more quickly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We don't have the legacy and the sort of history that slows us down, and we're glad to help.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There is no pricing sheet.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, there is. It's in a- it's in a briefcase and when you open it, it glows.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I would say, if you want this kind of transformation and you want to talk to us, it wouldn't be bad to get in touch soon, like not because we want your money, but just it's hectic out there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's hectic out there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And we do- we really want to talk to people who kind of do connect to this story and so if you're interested, get in touch, like it's- it's a good time and-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And if you have questions, we like questions. Podcast topics? hello@aboard.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sounds amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So Rich, our goal is to reach lots and lots of people with an inclusive and thoughtful message about how to build stuff with these new technologies, even when it is confusing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: All right.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I want more and more people to pay attention to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Share, like, subscribe, five stars, thumbs, all of it, every thumb you have, I need it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But anyway, it would be nice if you did it. Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All right friends. Well,

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Have a wonderful week.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yep. Don't worry, the boundaries are going to collapse, but we're still going to be nice.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You can't say 'don't worry, collapse'.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes you can.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, have a great week.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Bye.

highlights: AI is causing traditional professional boundaries to collapse as roles like designers, engineers, and product managers blur, allowing non-technical individuals to produce functional software artifacts.

The economic value of white-collar work has historically relied on the "expertise delta" between specialists and clients, a gap that is rapidly shrinking as AI enables non-experts to simulate high-level skills.
Many professionals are reacting defensively to AI because it challenges the specialized protocols and certifications that previously protected their market share and high-margin billing models.

Traditional labor-based pricing is becoming obsolete, requiring professionals to "self-commoditize" and shift their focus toward driving value through tool-driven efficiency rather than resisting the technology.
AI tools are drastically accelerating software development timelines, enabling the delivery of complex platforms in a fraction of the time and cost required by traditional agency models.
summary:
In this episode of the Aboard Podcast, hosts Paul Ford and Rich Ziade explore how artificial intelligence is rapidly dissolving traditional professional boundaries and business models. Ford highlights a "boundary collapse" where the once-distinct roles of designers, engineers, and product managers are merging as AI makes technical tasks like coding more accessible to non-engineers. Through humorous portmanteaus such as "clustomers" (client/customers) and "pragencies" (product/agencies), the hosts illustrate that the lines between products and services are blurring, forcing firms to rethink their identity and the way they deliver value.

The discussion also delves into the economic impact of AI on high-margin white-collar labor, specifically the shrinking "expertise gap." Ziade argues that professions like law and radiology have traditionally profited from the delta between an expert’s knowledge and a client’s lack thereof. As AI enables laypeople to perform complex tasks or receive high-level advice independently, professionals must move past defensive skepticism and learn to "self-commoditize." The hosts conclude that the future of professional value lies in embracing these tools to accelerate output, noting that their own firm has shifted from multi-month development cycles to 30- or 90-day AI-enabled launches that are significantly more cost-effective for clients.
description:
AI is blurring—and even destroying—the distinctions between disciplines. Do we need a new way to talk about work? On this week’s episode, Paul tests out a few of his AI-era neologisms on a skeptical Rich: Perhaps you are a “custolient,” looking to purchase the services of a “praygency” for your next project? (Yes, Paul insists the “y” in “praygency” is vital.) Are these new blended terms helpful, or just a way of talking around a very uncertain landscape? 

Captions are auto-generated; for audio, show notes, and a full transcript, visit our website: https://aboard.com/podcast/new-words-for-a-new-industry/
topics: The blurring of professional boundaries between designers, engineers, and product managers due to AI
The impact of AI on the value of white-collar expertise and labor
Changing economic and pricing models for services and agencies in the AI era
The acceleration of software development and shipping speeds using AI tools
